judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are thorough and nearly equivalent on error-handling, logging, and pseudonymization findings, but Report 1 explicitly states the rate limiter must not be disabled based on environment/stage-name value in addition to the Redis fail-open issue, while Report 2 calls the stage-based bypass "reasonable for dev," missing that half of the rate-limiter expectation.

report_1.evidence: ContentPolicyError → fallover. Wrong and the most serious... Concrete fix: replace the bare catch with an explicit denylist helper, e.g. NON_RETRYABLE = (BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError); in the except, if isinstance(e, NON_RETRYABLE): raise and only fall over on the rest (rate-limit/timeout/server/connection errors).

report_2.evidence: ContentPolicyError: inappropriate. A content-policy rejection is a deterministic property of the input; retrying on the secondary won't help... Concrete fix: catch only a defined set of retryable exceptions, e.g. except (RequestTimeoutError, ServerError, RateLimitError) as e: ... and let BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError propagate (or handle them explicitly as terminal).

report_1.evidence: Guardrail 3 says the limiter must fail closed everywhere except under a narrow, explicitly-named local override, and must not gate on an environment-name check... Fix: default to enforcing; only skip when a narrow, explicit override like RATELIMIT_DISABLED=1 is set for local dev. ... Path C — except RedisError: return True... Fails open — Guardrail 3 violation... Fix: return False (fail closed) on RedisError

report_2.evidence: Assessment: reasonable for dev, but note it means the limiter is fully disabled unless STAGE is exactly the string "production"... Fail-open by default is risky.

report_1.evidence: record.input_text — the full intake document text, verbatim... Concrete fix: log pseudonymize(record.applicant_id) instead of the raw id; do not log input_text (drop it, or log only a truncated SHA-256 digest for correlation); replace record.metadata with {k: record.metadata[k] for k in ALLOWED_METADATA_KEYS if k in record.metadata}

report_2.evidence: Concrete fix: never log input_text (log a length, hash, or document id instead); log pseudonymize(record.applicant_id) rather than the raw id; and scrub/allowlist metadata since it can also carry PII.

report_1.evidence: if PII_SALT is unset it silently falls back to an empty salt, producing an unsalted SHA-256 of the applicant id... Concrete fix: fail closed — salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"] or an explicit check that raises if the salt is missing/empty, so any route depending on pseudonymization refuses to run without the secret.

report_2.evidence: if PII_SALT is missing (or empty), the function silently degrades to an unsalted sha256(applicant_id)... it fails silently: there is no check that the salt is present... Concrete fix: require the salt in production (raise if PII_SALT is unset/empty rather than defaulting to "")
